skill.name: domain-modeling
skill.description: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.
skill.summary: Clarify project terminology, maintain context glossaries, and capture important architectural decisions.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown formatting, template text, and documentation instructions. The skill has a legitimate low-risk filesystem capability because it creates or updates CONTEXT.md and ADR files when domain terms or decisions are resolved.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown inline code, code fences, file paths, and examples. I found no executable scripts, shell invocation, or command construction in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations are Markdown examples and inline code. No runtime language file or executable command API is present.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are ordinary words inside Markdown descriptions and templates. The reconnaissance hits are documentation guidance such as scanning ADR numbers, not host inspection or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain prose, headings, and examples rather than cryptographic functions or system enumeration commands. I found no evidence of network access, secret access, or host reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Expected Filesystem Documentation Updates
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to create or update CONTEXT.md and ADR Markdown files. This is expected for the skill, but users should review generated documentation changes before committing them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple skill instructions explicitly direct documentation file creation or updates. The behavior is scoped to project Markdown files and is not malicious by itself.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when terms mean different things across code, planning, and documentation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code define shared language and record meaningful architecture decisions.
actual_capabilities: Identifies conflicting or overloaded domain terms during design discussions.
Maintains a concise CONTEXT.md glossary for single-context repositories.
Uses CONTEXT-MAP.md to work with multiple bounded contexts.
Creates ADRs only for hard-to-reverse and surprising decisions.
Cross-checks domain claims against code when implementation details matter.
Keeps implementation details out of the domain glossary.
limitations: It does not replace stakeholder validation of domain terms.
It does not generate full architecture specifications.
It requires repository access to update CONTEXT.md or ADR files.
It may ask clarifying questions when context boundaries are unclear.

use_cases.title: Align product and engineering language
use_cases.description: Create shared definitions for domain terms before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Document architectural trade-offs
use_cases.description: Record concise ADRs when a decision is costly to reverse and has meaningful alternatives.

use_cases.title: Clean up context boundaries
use_cases.description: Separate terms and relationships across multiple bounded contexts in a repository.

prompt_templates.title: Define a new domain term
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the domain-modeling skill to define the term 'Order' for this project. Ask me questions if the meaning is unclear.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve conflicting terminology
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the domain-modeling skill to compare how we use customer, user, and account. Propose canonical terms and update the glossary.

prompt_templates.title: Create an ADR for a design choice
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the domain-modeling skill to decide whether this database choice needs an ADR. If it qualifies, draft a concise ADR.

prompt_templates.title: Review bounded contexts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the domain-modeling skill to review our ordering and billing contexts. Identify unclear boundaries and suggest glossary updates.

output_examples.input: We use client, customer, and account for the same concept.
output_examples.output: The skill would ask which concept owns orders, then recommend one canonical term and list the avoided alternatives.

output_examples.input: We chose events instead of direct service calls between ordering and billing.
output_examples.output: The skill would test whether the choice is hard to reverse, surprising, and based on a real trade-off before drafting an ADR.

output_examples.input: Our repository has separate ordering and billing folders.
output_examples.output: The skill would look for a context map, then place glossary terms and ADRs in the matching context.

best_practices: Keep glossary definitions short and focused on business meaning.
Record ADRs only when future readers will need the reason.
Review documentation edits before committing them to the repository.
anti_patterns: Do not store implementation details in CONTEXT.md.
Do not create ADRs for obvious or easily reversed choices.
Do not accept vague terms when precise domain language is available.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates or updates CONTEXT.md, CONTEXT-MAP.md guidance, and ADR Markdown files when needed.

faq.question: Does it execute commands?
faq.answer: No evidence of command execution was found in the skill files.

faq.question: Can it work with one repository context?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses a root CONTEXT.md for most single-context repositories.

faq.question: Can it work with multiple contexts?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses CONTEXT-MAP.md to find and relate multiple contexts.

faq.question: When should an ADR be created?
faq.answer: Create one when a decision is hard to reverse, surprising without context, and based on a real trade-off.

faq.question: Who should review the output?
faq.answer: Engineers, product owners, and domain experts should review glossary terms and ADRs.
